channel.title: CWE-79 on ZAP
channel.description: Recent content in CWE-79 on ZAP

item.title: AngularJS $parse expression from cookie
item.description:
Cookie-controlled expression value reaches AngularJS $parse.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: AngularJS $parse expression from form input
item.description:
Form-controlled expression value reaches AngularJS $parse.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: AngularJS $parse expression from localStorage
item.description:
Storage-controlled expression value reaches AngularJS $parse.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: AngularJS $parse expression from postMessage
item.description:
postMessage-controlled expression value reaches AngularJS $parse.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: AngularJS expression executed through Function constructor
item.description:
Tainted data reached dynamic code execution while AngularJS expression parsing/compilation was active. This covers interpolation and $parse-style AngularJS expression injection cases.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: AngularJS expression injection - eval expression 1.4.0 to 1.4.9
item.description:
Detects AngularJS client-side template and expression injection by sending version-gated AngularJS sandbox-escape probes to query and form parameters, then requiring browser-executed proof from the PTK browser-nav harness.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.title: AngularJS expression injection - expression 1.0.1 to 1.1.5
item.description:
Detects AngularJS client-side template and expression injection by sending version-gated AngularJS sandbox-escape probes to query and form parameters, then requiring browser-executed proof from the PTK browser-nav harness.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.description:

item.description:
Detects AngularJS client-side template and expression injection by sending version-gated AngularJS sandbox-escape probes to query and form parameters, then requiring browser-executed proof from the PTK browser-nav harness.

item.title: AngularJS expression injection - single-quote expression 1.2.19 to 1.2.23
item.description:
Detects AngularJS client-side template and expression injection by sending version-gated AngularJS sandbox-escape probes to query and form parameters, then requiring browser-executed proof from the PTK browser-nav harness.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.title: AngularJS interpolation delimiters in template string
item.description:
Finds AngularJS code patterns where untrusted data is compiled or parsed as AngularJS expressions/templates, including $parse, $interpolate, $compile, interpolation delimiters and ng-* expression attributes.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: AngularJS ng-* expression attribute
item.description:
Finds AngularJS code patterns where untrusted data is compiled or parsed as AngularJS expressions/templates, including $parse, $interpolate, $compile, interpolation delimiters and ng-* expression attributes.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: AngularJS template injection - alternate delimiters 1.6 and later
item.description:

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.description:
Detects AngularJS client-side template and expression injection by sending version-gated AngularJS sandbox-escape probes to query and form parameters, then requiring browser-executed proof from the PTK browser-nav harness.

item.title: AngularJS template injection - reflected short legacy 1.0.1 to 1.1.5
item.description:
Detects AngularJS client-side template and expression injection by sending version-gated AngularJS sandbox-escape probes to query and form parameters, then requiring browser-executed proof from the PTK browser-nav harness.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.title: Cross Site Scripting (DOM Based)
item.description: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user’s browser instance. A browser instance can be a standard web browser client, or a browser object embedded in a software product such as the browser within WinAmp, an RSS reader, or an email client. The code itself is usually written in HTML/JavaScript, but may also extend to VBScript, ActiveX, Java, Flash, or any other browser-supported technology. When an attacker gets a user’s browser to execute his/her code, the code will run within the security context (or zone) of the hosting web site. With this level of privilege, the code has the ability to read, modify and transmit any sensitive data accessible by the browser. A Cross-site Scripted user could have his/her account hijacked (cookie theft), their browser redirected to another location, or possibly shown fraudulent content delivered by the web site they are visiting. Cross-site Scripting attacks essentially compromise the trust relationship between a user and the web site. Applications utilizing browser object instances which load content from the file system may execute code under the local machine zone allowing for system compromise.

item.title: Cross Site Scripting (Persistent)
item.description: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user’s browser instance. A browser instance can be a standard web browser client, or a browser object embedded in a software product such as the browser within WinAmp, an RSS reader, or an email client. The code itself is usually written in HTML/JavaScript, but may also extend to VBScript, ActiveX, Java, Flash, or any other browser-supported technology. When an attacker gets a user’s browser to execute his/her code, the code will run within the security context (or zone) of the hosting web site. With this level of privilege, the code has the ability to read, modify and transmit any sensitive data accessible by the browser. A Cross-site Scripted user could have his/her account hijacked (cookie theft), their browser redirected to another location, or possibly shown fraudulent content delivered by the web site they are visiting. Cross-site Scripting attacks essentially compromise the trust relationship between a user and the web site. Applications utilizing browser object instances which load content from the file system may execute code under the local machine zone allowing for system compromise.

item.title: Cross Site Scripting (Persistent)
item.description: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user’s browser instance. A browser instance can be a standard web browser client, or a browser object embedded in a software product such as the browser within WinAmp, an RSS reader, or an email client. The code itself is usually written in HTML/JavaScript, but may also extend to VBScript, ActiveX, Java, Flash, or any other browser-supported technology. When an attacker gets a user’s browser to execute his/her code, the code will run within the security context (or zone) of the hosting web site. With this level of privilege, the code has the ability to read, modify and transmit any sensitive data accessible by the browser. A Cross-site Scripted user could have his/her account hijacked (cookie theft), their browser redirected to another location, or possibly shown fraudulent content delivered by the web site they are visiting. Cross-site Scripting attacks essentially compromise the trust relationship between a user and the web site. Applications utilizing browser object instances which load content from the file system may execute code under the local machine zone allowing for system compromise.

item.title: Cross Site Scripting (Reflected)
item.description: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user’s browser instance. A browser instance can be a standard web browser client, or a browser object embedded in a software product such as the browser within WinAmp, an RSS reader, or an email client. The code itself is usually written in HTML/JavaScript, but may also extend to VBScript, ActiveX, Java, Flash, or any other browser-supported technology. When an attacker gets a user’s browser to execute his/her code, the code will run within the security context (or zone) of the hosting web site. With this level of privilege, the code has the ability to read, modify and transmit any sensitive data accessible by the browser. A Cross-site Scripted user could have his/her account hijacked (cookie theft), their browser redirected to another location, or possibly shown fraudulent content delivered by the web site they are visiting. Cross-site Scripting attacks essentially compromise the trust relationship between a user and the web site. Applications utilizing browser object instances which load content from the file system may execute code under the local machine zone allowing for system compromise.

item.title: Cross Site Scripting Weakness (Persistent in JSON Response)
item.description: A XSS attack was found in a JSON response, this might leave content consumers vulnerable to attack if they don’t appropriately handle the data (response).

item.title: Cross-site Scripting
item.description: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user’s browser instance. A browser instance can be a standard web browser client, or a browser object embedded in a software product such as the browser within WinAmp, an RSS reader, or an email client. The code itself is usually written in HTML/JavaScript, but may also extend to VBScript, ActiveX, Java, Flash, or any other browser-supported technology. When an attacker gets a user’s browser to execute his/her code, the code will run within the security context (or zone) of the hosting web site. With this level of privilege, the code has the ability to read, modify and transmit any sensitive data accessible by the browser. A Cross-site Scripted user could have his/her account hijacked (cookie theft), their browser redirected to another location, or possibly shown fraudulent content delivered by the web site they are visiting. Cross-site Scripting attacks essentially compromise the trust relationship between a user and the web site. Applications utilizing browser object instances which load content from the file system may execute code under the local machine zone allowing for system compromise.

item.title: Cross-site Scripting
item.description: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is an attack technique that involves echoing attacker-supplied code into a user’s browser instance. A browser instance can be a standard web browser client, or a browser object embedded in a software product such as the browser within WinAmp, an RSS reader, or an email client. The code itself is usually written in HTML/JavaScript, but may also extend to VBScript, ActiveX, Java, Flash, or any other browser-supported technology. When an attacker gets a user’s browser to execute his/her code, the code will run within the security context (or zone) of the hosting web site. With this level of privilege, the code has the ability to read, modify and transmit any sensitive data accessible by the browser. A Cross-site Scripted user could have his/her account hijacked (cookie theft), their browser redirected to another location, or possibly shown fraudulent content delivered by the web site they are visiting. Cross-site Scripting attacks essentially compromise the trust relationship between a user and the web site. Applications utilizing browser object instances which load content from the file system may execute code under the local machine zone allowing for system compromise.

item.title: Disallow document.write()/writeln()
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Disallow innerHTML/outerHTML assignments
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Disallow insertAdjacentHTML()
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: DOM XSS via document.write
item.description:
Tainted data passed to document.write.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: DOM XSS via document.write (secondary sources)
item.description:
Persisted/reflected client-side values reached document.write.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: DOM XSS via DOM mutation (secondary sources)
item.description:
Persisted/reflected client-side values reached mutation sinks.

item.title: DOM XSS via DOM mutations
item.description:
Tainted data inserted into the DOM via DOM mutation APIs.

item.title: DOM XSS via DOMParser.parseFromString
item.description:
Tainted HTML parsed through DOMParser.parseFromString with an HTML-like MIME type.

item.title: DOM XSS via Element.innerHTML
item.description:
Tainted data assigned to innerHTML (possible DOM XSS).

Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

item.title: DOM XSS via query param attribute breakout
item.description:
Tests top-level GET query parameters for browser-executed XSS by opening a real browser attack tab and requiring an execution marker after load or safe synthetic interaction.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.description:
Tests top-level GET query parameters for browser-executed XSS by opening a real browser attack tab and requiring an execution marker after load or safe synthetic interaction.

item.title: DOM-based XSS (taint flow)
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Dynamic AngularJS $compile/$interpolate template
item.description:
Finds AngularJS code patterns where untrusted data is compiled or parsed as AngularJS expressions/templates, including $parse, $interpolate, $compile, interpolation delimiters and ng-* expression attributes.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Dynamic AngularJS $parse expression
item.description:
Finds AngularJS code patterns where untrusted data is compiled or parsed as AngularJS expressions/templates, including $parse, $interpolate, $compile, interpolation delimiters and ng-* expression attributes.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Inline event handler built from dynamic data
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: JSONP callback parameter controls JavaScript response
item.description:
Tests callback-like parameters for JSONP-style JavaScript responses where user input controls the executed callback name.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.title: Reflected HTTP GET Parameter(s)
item.description: A reflected parameter value has been found in the HTTP response. Reflected parameter values may introduce XSS vulnerability or HTTP header injection.

item.title: Response field parsed via createContextualFragment
item.description:
Response-derived HTML parsed via Range.createContextualFragment.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field parsed via DOMParser
item.description:
Response-derived HTML parsed via DOMParser.parseFromString.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via document.write
item.description:
Response-derived data reached document.write.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via DOM mutation
item.description:
Response-derived data reached DOM mutation sinks.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via innerHTML
item.description:
Response-derived data reached innerHTML.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via insertAdjacentHTML
item.description:
Response-derived HTML reached insertAdjacentHTML.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via outerHTML
item.description:
Response-derived data reached outerHTML.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via setHTMLUnsafe
item.description:
Response-derived HTML reached Element.setHTMLUnsafe.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Response field rendered via ShadowRoot.setHTMLUnsafe
item.description:
Response-derived HTML reached ShadowRoot.setHTMLUnsafe.

Generated by OWASP PTK IAST Module

item.title: Review DOMParser.parseFromString with dynamic HTML/XML
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Review uses of appendChild
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: SPA hash DOM XSS
item.description:
Tests hash-based SPA parameters (http://host/#/route?param=…) for DOM XSS by mutating the hash in a dedicated attack tab and inspecting the DOM.

Generated by OWASP PTK DAST Module

item.title: Tainted data compiled as AngularJS template
item.description:
Finds AngularJS code patterns where untrusted data is compiled or parsed as AngularJS expressions/templates, including $parse, $interpolate, $compile, interpolation delimiters and ng-* expression attributes.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: Tainted data passed to AngularJS $parse
item.description:
Finds AngularJS code patterns where untrusted data is compiled or parsed as AngularJS expressions/templates, including $parse, $interpolate, $compile, interpolation delimiters and ng-* expression attributes.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: template.innerHTML with dynamic content
item.description:
Detects cases where untrusted data from the DOM (URL, element values, storage, messages, etc.) flows into HTML/JS execution sinks (e.g., innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, string-based setTimeout, insertAdjacentHTML) without proper sanitization or encoding \u2014 enabling DOM-based cross-site scripting.

Generated by OWASP PTK SAST Module

item.title: XSS - attribute context img onerror
item.description: Reflected Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is another name for non-persistent XSS, where the attack doesn’t load with the vulnerable web application but is originated by the victim loading the offending URI. In this article we will see some ways to test a web application for this kind of vulnerability.

item.title: XSS - attribute-name event injection
item.description: Reflected Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is another name for non-persistent XSS, where the attack doesn’t load with the vulnerable web application but is originated by the victim loading the offending URI. In this article we will see some ways to test a web application for this kind of vulnerability.

item.title: XSS - double-quoted attribute event injection
item.description: Reflected Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is another name for non-persistent XSS, where the attack doesn’t load with the vulnerable web application but is originated by the victim loading the offending URI. In this article we will see some ways to test a web application for this kind of vulnerability.

item.description: Reflected Cross-site Scripting (XSS) is another name for non-persistent XSS, where the attack doesn’t load with the vulnerable web application but is originated by the victim loading the offending URI. In this article we will see some ways to test a web application for this kind of vulnerability.

In this article we will see some ways to test a web application for this kind of vulnerability.
